title: Role of mass fluctuations in the diffusion of clusters of Brownian particles with activity
authors: Daniela Moretti, Pasquale Digregorio, Giuseppe Gonnella, Antonio Suma
abstract: Motivated by the anomalous diffusion observed in clusters of active Brownian particles (ABPs), where the center-of-mass diffusion coefficient scales as $D\sim N^{-1/2}$ with respect to the number $N$ of particles in the cluster, we derive a minimal theoretical framework starting from the single-particle Langevin equations. The model consists of two coupled stochastic equations: one for the cluster center-of-mass trajectory and one for the mass evolution $N(t)$, explicitly accounting for stochastic displacements induced by particle attachment and detachment. We specialize and validate the framework against ABP simulations of isolated clusters in stationary conditions, where $N(t)$ follows a Gaussian process with mean $N_0$, variance $\propto N_0^β$, and persistence time $\propto N_0^κ$. Analytical solution of the coupled equations yields the long-time diffusion coefficient as the sum of two contributions: a conventional term $\propto N_0^{-1}$) due to thermal noise plus summation of active forces, and a fluctuation-driven term $\propto N_0^{-δ}$ with $δ=2-2/d-β+κ$, where $d$ is the spatial dimension. We demonstrate that anomalous scaling emerges whenever the second term becomes dominant. The model predicts $D\sim N^{-α}$ with $α=0.63\pm0.06$, in good quantitative agreement with large-scale ABP simulations.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical explanation for the anomalous diffusion of active particle clusters by accounting for the stochastic fluctuations in cluster mass due to particle attachment and detachment. It demonstrates that these mass fluctuations create a new diffusion contribution that dominates at large cluster sizes, leading to the observed non-standard scaling.
main_problem: Explaining the origin of anomalous diffusion in the center-of-mass of clusters of Active Brownian Particles (ABPs), specifically why the diffusion coefficient scales as D ~ N^(-1/2) instead of the conventional N^(-1).
main_result: The authors derive a theoretical framework showing that the diffusion coefficient is the sum of a conventional term and a fluctuation-driven term arising from mass exchange, predicting an anomalous scaling exponent alpha = 0.63 +/- 0.06 that matches simulations.
method: The study combines an analytical derivation using coupled stochastic Langevin equations with large-scale numerical simulations of Active Brownian Particles.
model_or_system: The system consists of clusters of Active Brownian Particles (ABPs) undergoing Motility-induced Phase Separation (MIPS), modeled using Active Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Particles (AOUPs) to account for mass exchange (attachment/detachment) with the surrounding phase.
key_observables: Center-of-mass diffusion coefficient (D), Mean-Square Displacement (MSD), cluster mass evolution N(t), and scaling exponents (alpha, beta, kappa, delta).
important_parameters: Mean cluster size (N0), mass fluctuation variance scaling (beta), mass fluctuation persistence time (kappa), and spatial dimension (d).
main_assumptions: The mass evolution N(t) follows a Gaussian process; clusters are assumed to be roughly spherical; mass fluctuations are small relative to the total mass (|delta N| < N).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows cluster dynamics and mass exchange snapshots; Figure 2 illustrates the schematic of fragment attachment/detachment and fits for the diffusion exponents; Figure 3 shows the diffusion coefficient scaling with cluster size in 2D.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of anomalous diffusion, presents a theoretical framework based on coupled stochastic equations for COM and mass, validates the model against ABP simulations, and discusses the scaling regimes and dimensionality effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Time evolution of a stationary cluster of N(t) particles, taken from an ABPs simulation with a total of 3025 particles (cluster mean size N0 = 2036 as black line) and (b) corresponding probability distribution P(N), see SM for simulation details. The yellow shaded region marks the range where N fluctuates within 10% of N0; within this region P(N) is approximately Gaussian (black line fit in (b)). (c) Snapshots at two consecutive close times (left to right) with particles lost from the left snapshots highlighted in red; top (bottom) row shows a mass-change event inside (outside) the yellow region of (a). (d) Cluster’s diffusion coefficient D versus mean cluster size N0 from ABP...

Figure 2: (a) Schematic of fragment attachment to the main cluster. The fragment (center-of-mass r∆N CM) and the main cluster (center-of-mass rN CM) are connected by the vector dcm = |dcm|ζ, where ζ is a random unit vector drawn from the surface of a d-dimensional sphere and |dcm| equals the sum of the two radii. Projections of the centers of mass along an arbitrary direction rCM,a are also shown. (b) Rescaled probability distributions P(N) from ABP simulations at different mean cluster sizes N0. The solid line is the standard normal distribution N(0, 1). (c) Variance σ2 N versus mean cluster size N0, extracted from the distributions in (b). Figure 3: Diffusion coefficient D versus mean cluster size N0, as given by Eq. (18), using Da = 8.33, d = 2 (same value as ABPs simulations, see SM). (a) Fixed Dg = 1.4 (from fitted ABPs simulation values); and different values of δ. (b) Fixed δ = 0.63 and different values of Dg.
